content: context window. They don't see each other's feedback. They cite specific file:line references with severity tags: Critical, Important, Low. The HNSW index rebuild review caught 2 criticals and 5 other issues in a single pass. The real output is on slide 6. The PM reviewer caught something no linter ever would: I'd fixed a blocking I/O problem in 2 of 7 call sites and was about to ship it as done. The PM flagged it as incomplete. That's not a code bug. That's a "you thought you were finished" bug. The reviews also run after the design phase, not just after code. A spec review caught that I was about to implement a feature in the wrong file entirely. Another generated 13 implementation issues from an RFC before any code existed. Slide 8 has the full breakdown. Out of 323 commits on this project, 42 are fixing things the reviewers caught. Roughly 1 in 8 commits exists because a reviewer flagged something I missed. It adds about 2 minutes to each commit. The system isn't perfect. Reviews are sometimes overcautious, and I cap it at three fix iterations. But the shift is real: the leverage isn't in writing code faster. It's in simulating the team dynamics that catch mistakes before they ship. What's the best bug your AI reviewer caught that you would have shipped? Curious what setups people have landed on. #AICodeReview #ClaudeCode #CodeQuality #DevWorkflow #SoloDev
